growing_conditions.notes: Full sun required; humidity above 80% increases Alternaria and thrips risk; grows best at 0–450 masl in lowland tropical Indonesia

actions: Soak bulbs in fungicide solution (mancozeb) before planting
Apply mancozeb spray at 7 DAP if humidity is high
Monitor for bulb rot (Fusarium) — rogue infected plants immediately
Apply systemic insecticide (imidacloprid) if thrips exceed 5 per clump
Preventive contact fungicide every 5 days during rain
Remove and destroy wilting plants immediately
Stop insecticide spray 2 weeks before harvest
Apply protective fungicide against anthracnose
Reduce spray frequency during dry conditions
No specific pruning required for shallots
Manual weeding every 2 weeks
Remove yellowing or rotting leaves immediately

watering_schedule.notes: Frequent shallow watering drives rooting; never waterlog the raised bed

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — ~5–6 mm/day, wet root zone to ~15 cm

Ease off water as bulbs size up to prevent bulb rot
Stop irrigation 7–10 days before harvest to cure and firm bulbs

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at planting
Promote leaf growth
Stop nitrogen fertilizer 2 weeks before harvest

soil_parameters.notes: Gradually reduce irrigation to prevent bulb rot

growth_benchmarks.notes: Leaves should be fresh green
Swelling at stem base should be visible
Bulbs clearly formed, leaves starting to lodge — harvest approaching

crops.name: Shallot (True Seed / TSS)
crops.notes: Grown from True Seed of Shallot (TSS). Nursery phase (30–45 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date. Produces larger bulbs with higher yield than bulb-planted shallot.

growing_conditions.notes: Full sun required; humidity above 80% increases disease pressure; TSS cultivar performs better at mid-elevation (200–600 masl); similar conditions to bulb-planted shallot

actions: Apply mancozeb spray at 7 DAP if humidity is high
Monitor for thrips; apply imidacloprid at >5 insects per clump
Remove and destroy wilting transplants immediately
Apply systemic insecticide if thrips or caterpillar pressure increases
Preventive contact fungicide every 5–7 days during wet weather
Monitor for Alternaria leaf blight — spray copper fungicide at first sign
Stop insecticide spray 2 weeks before harvest
Apply protective fungicide against anthracnose
Gradually reduce irrigation to prevent bulb rot
No specific pruning required
Manual weeding every 2 weeks
Remove yellowing or rotting leaves immediately

watering_schedule.notes: Keep transplants moist until they recover and root out
Steady moisture builds larger TSS bulbs; avoid drought stress
Gradually reduce irrigation to firm bulbs and limit rot
Stop irrigation 7–10 days before harvest to cure bulbs

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting into main field
Promote leaf and tiller growth after transplant
Stop nitrogen fertilizer 2 weeks before harvest; potassium for bulb sizing

soil_parameters.notes: Well-drained, fertile soil preferred; raised beds 20–30 cm high recommended
Reduce irrigation gradually to firm up bulbs; avoid waterlogging

growth_benchmarks.notes: Post-transplant establishment; active vegetative growth
Bulb swelling visible at stem base
TSS bulbs are typically larger than bulb-planted shallots; leaves lodging — harvest imminent

growing_conditions.notes: Highland conditions (>700 masl) essential for bulb development in Indonesia; cool temperatures during clove formation are critical — lowland production results in severely poor bulbing

actions: Treat seed cloves with fungicide (mancozeb / thiram) before planting
Monitor for Fusarium root rot — rogue infected plants immediately
Apply insecticide if early thrips attack detected
Spray protective fungicide every 5–7 days
Monitor for downy mildew during rain or high humidity
Apply contact insecticide if thrips population increases
Gradually reduce fungicide applications
Stop insecticide 3 weeks before harvest
Maintain air circulation between beds
No specific pruning required
Regular weeding every 2 weeks
Remove scapes (false flower stalks) promptly to redirect energy to cloves

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — keep bed evenly moist, ~4–5 mm/day equivalent
watering_schedule.notes: Cool highland soil; keep moist for sprouting but never waterlogged (basal rot risk)

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — wet root zone to ~20 cm
watering_schedule.notes: Consistent moisture through leaf growth; drainage is critical
Taper water during bulbing to improve clove quality and storage life
Withhold water 2–3 weeks before harvest once leaves start yellowing

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at planting; dolomite corrects pH and adds Mg & S
Push vegetative growth; sulfur from ZA benefits allium flavor
Switch to potassium-dominant nutrition for clove sizing

soil_parameters.notes: Well-drained loamy soil preferred; avoid waterlogging which causes basal rot

growth_benchmarks.notes: Leaves should be dark green and erect
Maximum leaf canopy before scape removal

crops.name: Celery
crops.notes: Nursery phase (40–60 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

growing_conditions.notes: Cool highland climate required (>700 masl in Indonesia); 30–50% shade cloth recommended in tropical conditions to prevent heat stress and leaf curl; consistent moisture critical to prevent stringy stalks

actions: Apply protective fungicide (mancozeb) every 7 days if humidity is high
Monitor for early blight; spray copper-based fungicide at first symptoms
Use row covers or netting first 4–5 weeks to reduce pest pressure
Stop insecticide 2 weeks before harvest
Ensure calcium supply to prevent black heart disorder
Reduce overhead irrigation to limit fungal splash
Remove outer yellowing or damaged petioles to improve airflow
Tie stalks loosely if sprawling to keep upright form
Weed shallowly — celery has shallow roots, avoid deep cultivation
Bank soil or use paper sleeves around base to blanch stalks (optional for pale, tender stalks)
Remove any bolting flower stems immediately

watering_schedule.amount: Light and frequent — keep surface constantly moist
watering_schedule.notes: Shallow-rooted; must never dry out after transplant

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — ~6 mm/day, keep soil at 70–85% moisture
watering_schedule.notes: Consistent moisture prevents stringy, hollow stalks; use mulch to retain water

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate to high — do not let soil dry
watering_schedule.notes: Reduce overhead irrigation to limit leaf fungal splash; keep root zone evenly wet

fertilization_schedule.notes: Incorporate into field before transplanting; high phosphorus supports root establishment
Nitrogen push to drive leaf & stalk mass
Calcium critical to prevent black heart; reduce nitrogen to avoid splitting

soil_parameters.notes: Celery needs consistently moist, organically rich soil; drought causes stringy stalks

growth_benchmarks.notes: Post-transplant establishment; should show active new growth
Stalk development beginning; should be upright and compact
Harvest approaching; outer stalks ready when ≥30 cm

growing_conditions.notes: Full sun promotes vine and root growth; drought-tolerant once established but consistent moisture needed during root bulking (DAP 50–80); light well-drained ridged soil essential

actions: Use vine tip cuttings (25 cm) from certified disease-free mother plants
Dip cuttings in fungicide solution before planting to prevent Fusarium
Apply soil insecticide (carbofuran) at planting to suppress wireworms and weevil larvae
Spray fenitrothion 0.5% at 30 DAP and again at 50 DAP for weevil control
Monitor for whitefly virus vectors; use yellow sticky traps
Hilling at 25–30 DAP covers developing roots, reducing weevil entry
Final fenitrothion spray at 70 DAP if weevil pressure remains high
Stop all insecticide 3 weeks before harvest
Reduce irrigation to harden roots before harvest
Lift and redirect vines every 2–3 weeks to prevent adventitious rooting away from ridge
Cut and remove excess vines to maintain airflow and reduce disease
Weed thoroughly at 10–12 DAP and again at 25–30 DAP

watering_schedule.notes: Keep vine cuttings moist until they root and start new growth
Avoid excess water, which pushes vines over roots

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — consistent moisture, ~5 mm per application
watering_schedule.notes: Most water-sensitive stage; steady moisture drives root sizing, but avoid waterlogging
Withhold water 2–3 weeks before harvest to harden roots and improve storage

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply in furrows at planting; avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes vines over roots
Phosphorus and potassium encourage root initiation
Potassium-heavy side-dress at bulking stage; do NOT add nitrogen

pests.action_threshold: adult weevils or entry tunnels in vines found

pests.name: Fusarium stem rot (Fusarium solani)
pests.action_threshold: internal browning of vine base found

pests.action_threshold: tunneling found in >2% of sampled roots

Soil surface should show early root swelling near crown
Roots should be uniform in shape; check by sampling before full harvest

crops.name: Spring Onion
crops.notes: Nursery phase (20–30 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

growing_conditions.notes: Tolerates partial shade in hot weather; grows year-round in Indonesian lowlands; high humidity increases purple blotch and downy mildew risk

actions: Treat seeds or seedling roots with fungicide (thiram) before transplanting
Apply mulch to maintain soil moisture and suppress weeds
Monitor for damping-off in nursery; reduce watering if stems thin at base

Ensure good drainage to prevent purple blotch
Weed manually every 10–14 days; spring onions compete poorly with weeds
Remove dead or yellowing outer leaves to encourage new growth
No topping required unless harvesting leaf portion selectively

Steady moisture drives fast, tender leaf elongation; good drainage prevents purple blotch
Stop watering 2–3 days before harvest to keep leaves crisp

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply before transplanting; mix well into soil
Nitrogen-focused to drive fast leaf elongation
Balanced nutrition; stop 1 week before harvest

pests.name: Downy mildew (Peronospora destructor)
pests.action_threshold: pale green/yellow patches with gray fuzz found

soil_parameters.notes: Loamy soil with good drainage; avoid clay-heavy soils

growth_benchmarks.notes: Transplant shock recovery complete; active new leaf growth
Rapid leaf elongation phase
Ready for first selective harvest of outer leaves

crops.name: Chili Pepper
crops.notes: Nursery phase (21–25 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

growing_conditions.notes: Full sun essential; high humidity (>80%) during vegetative phase promotes thrips and Fusarium wilt; Above 32°C causes flower drop and poor fruit set; humidity above 85% promotes anthracnose on fruit; harvest every 3–5 days to maintain productivity

actions: Dip transplant roots in Trichoderma + Imidacloprid 0.5 ml/L for 15 min before transplanting
Apply systemic insecticide (imidacloprid) if thrips or virus vectors detected
Protective fungicide every 5–7 days in wet weather
Rotate contact and systemic fungicides to prevent resistance
Monitor for yellow leaf curl virus (Begomovirus); remove infected plants
Use selective insecticides to preserve beneficial insects
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Continue protective fungicide against anthracnose
Harvest regularly every 3–5 days to maintain plant productivity
Remove all suckers below the first V-shaped branching
Strip lower leaves up to 20 cm from ground to improve airflow
Remove non-productive lateral branches
Remove first-set fruits (too early) to strengthen the main stem
Optional topping for overly tall plants
Remove diseased or dried branches immediately
Remove rotting fruits on the plant to prevent spread

watering_schedule.amount: Moderate — keep root zone moist, ~4–5 mm/day
watering_schedule.notes: Steady moisture helps transplants establish; avoid waterlogging (Phytophthora/Fusarium)
Avoid moisture stress at flowering to prevent flower drop; keep even to reduce blossom-end rot
Keep moisture steady to size fruit and prevent cracking and BER; water at base, not overhead

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting
Promote vegetative growth
Boron critical for fruit set
Calcium prevents blossom end rot
Stop nitrogen; focus on potassium for heat level and shelf life

pests.action_threshold: dropped fruits or small entry holes found

soil_parameters.notes: Maintain calcium to prevent BER; avoid drought stress during fruit set
